title: Metamerism: the trapdoor under every color match
intro: Pour two pools of warm-white light onto a wall, side by side, and watch them behave as one. The first is a tungsten filament: a smooth continuum from 380 nm to 780 nm peaked deep in the red. The second is a phosphor white LED: a narrow blue spike at 450 nm and a broad orange-yellow hump near 580 nm, with almost nothing in between. On a spectrometer the two spectra are nothing alike. On the wall they are indistinguishable. The previous lesson on the CIE 1931 chromaticity diagram called the (x, y) sheet honest about identity: the same (x, y) means the same perceived color. This lesson opens the trapdoor under that honesty. Infinitely many different spectra map to a single (X, Y, Z), and the consequences land squarely on the paint store, the camera, and every match made under one light and judged under another.

sections.heading: 1. Helmholtz's matched pigments and the word he gave them

blocks.text: Hermann von Helmholtz, the Berlin physiologist who in the 1850s and 1860s wrote the three-volume Handbuch der physiologischen Optik that taught the next century how the eye worked, set down the phenomenon as plainly as it deserved. Pigment makers and dyers had known it for generations. A pair of greens carefully matched under the candlelight of an evening could betray each other under the gray of a London afternoon. The match was not a bad match. The match was a conditional match, and the condition was the light in the room.

Helmholtz called the agreeing pair a metamer, from the Greek meta (after, beyond) and meros (part, share). The same root gives the chemist polymer and isomer. Two metameric stimuli share an outcome (the same perceived color) by way of different parts (different spectral shapes). The word stuck because the phenomenon stuck. Anywhere the eye collapses a continuous spectrum into a few channels, and anywhere two different continuous spectra fall through those channels to the same readings, a metamer is the result.

The temptation, the first time one meets metamerism, is to call it a perceptual illusion. It is not. The eye is not failing to distinguish two spectra it could in principle separate. It is structurally incapable of separating them, in the same way a black-and-white camera is structurally incapable of distinguishing red from green. Three channels in, infinitely many spectra out the other side. Everything Helmholtz wrote down, and everything the chromaticity diagram quietly admitted by labeling its sheet a two-dimensional projection of color, follows from that one count.

sections.heading: 2. Three integrals, three constraints, infinitely many spectra

blocks.text: What the eye reduces the spectrum to was measured in two parallel experiments between 1928 and 1931, by William David Wright at Imperial College London and John Guild at the National Physical Laboratory in Teddington. Their procedure was the color-matching experiment the previous lesson described: dial three primary lights until the mixture matches a monochromatic test. The CIE 1931 standard observer, the linear remix of Wright and Guild's averaged data that keeps every matching coefficient positive, summarizes the eye's response in three smooth non-negative curves x̄(λ), ȳ(λ), z̄(λ), the color matching functions. Direct measurement of the cone photopigments underneath them came fifty years later, by microspectrophotometry (the technique of shining a microscope-scale beam through a single dissected cone outer segment and recording its absorption spectrum), and confirmed three pigments peaking near 564, 533, and 437 nm. Either way, three numbers reach the brain.
The three numbers are linear integrals of the spectrum against the three matching functions, the formula carried over from the chromaticity lesson:

blocks.tex: X = \int S(\lambda)\,\bar{x}(\lambda)\,d\lambda, \quad Y = \int S(\lambda)\,\bar{y}(\lambda)\,d\lambda, \quad Z = \int S(\lambda)\,\bar{z}(\lambda)\,d\lambda
blocks.caption: S(λ) is the spectral power distribution of the light reaching the eye, the power per unit wavelength at each wavelength. λ is wavelength. x̄(λ), ȳ(λ), z̄(λ) are the three CIE 1931 color matching functions, tabulated at 5 nm intervals across 380 to 780 nm. X, Y, and Z are the three tristimulus values that summarize the spectrum's effect on the standard observer. The integrals run over the visible band.

blocks.text: Two spectra S₁(λ) and S₂(λ) that produce the same (X, Y, Z) cannot be told apart by the standard observer. By linearity of the integral, subtracting one from the other gives the metamer condition:

blocks.caption: S₁(λ) and S₂(λ) are two candidate spectra under test as a metameric pair. Their difference S₁ − S₂ must integrate to zero against each of the three matching functions. x̄, ȳ, z̄ are the CIE matching functions from the previous block. The three integral equations carve out a codimension-3 subspace of the infinite-dimensional space of spectra, and every difference S₁ − S₂ in that subspace is undetectable to the standard observer.

blocks.text: The space of spectra is infinite-dimensional and the constraints are three. The solution set is infinite-dimensional. Any function orthogonal to all three matching functions in the integral above is a black metamer, sometimes called a fundamental metamer: a perturbation invisible to the eye. Add one to any reference spectrum and the color stays put. Subtract one and likewise. The eye operates with a vast structural blind spot built into its geometry, and a chunk of the rest of color science is the engineering project of navigating around it.

quiz.question: A vendor of synthetic dyes claims to have developed an indigo pigment whose spectral reflectance is genuinely unique: no other pigment in the world produces the same (X, Y, Z) under D65 daylight (the noon-daylight illuminant standard). The reflectance is tabulated and the (X, Y, Z) under D65 is given. Assuming the standard CIE 1931 observer and broadband daylight, is the vendor's uniqueness claim defensible?
options: Yes. The CIE matching functions x̄(λ), ȳ(λ), z̄(λ) form a basis of the space of visible spectra, so each (X, Y, Z) triple corresponds to exactly one reflectance.

No. The eye integrates the spectrum linearly against three matching functions and returns three numbers, so the map from spectra to (X, Y, Z) collapses an infinite-dimensional space onto a 3-dimensional one. Its kernel is infinite-dimensional, and every (X, Y, Z) is the image of an infinite family of reflectances. Any other dye chemist can in principle synthesize a different pigment that produces the same (X, Y, Z) under D65; what the indigo lacks may be metamerism failure under other illuminants, not uniqueness under this one.
Yes, but only for monochromatic stimuli; broadband pigments are uniquely fixed by their (X, Y, Z) only when their bandwidth is below 5 nm, the CIE tabulation interval.
No, but only because in practice no two dye chemists can manufacture identical (X, Y, Z); the theoretical uniqueness holds, the practical uniqueness does not.
quiz.explanation: Option 1 mistakes the matching functions for a basis of the spectrum space. Three smooth non-negative functions cannot span an infinite-dimensional function space; they project it onto a 3-dimensional output, and three integrals against three fixed kernels leave a codimension-3 kernel containing every function orthogonal to all three. Option 3 invents a bandwidth dependence that is not in the math: the integrals are over the spectrum, however narrow or broad. Option 4 conflates a manufacturing limit with a mathematical one. The honest answer is option 2. The map S(λ) → (X, Y, Z) is a linear projection from an infinite-dimensional space onto R³. Its null space is infinite-dimensional, and every output triple is the image of an infinite family of inputs. The indigo's interesting property, if any, would be that its illuminant-metamerism failures (the topic of section 4) under a different illuminant set it apart from a competitor that matched under D65 but diverged under a phosphor LED.

blocks.text: The infinite-dimensional kernel is concrete enough to manufacture by hand. Pick a reference spectrum and compute its target (X*, Y*, Z*). Pick three peak wavelengths λ_b, λ_g, λ_r in the blue, green, and red bands of the visible range (in the viz: 460 nm, 540 nm, 600 nm). Solve for three peak heights w_b, w_g, w_r such that three narrow spikes at the chosen wavelengths produce the same (X*, Y*, Z*) the reference does. The system is three linear equations in three unknowns:

blocks.tex: \begin{aligned} w_b\,\bar{x}(\lambda_b) + w_g\,\bar{x}(\lambda_g) + w_r\,\bar{x}(\lambda_r) &= X^{*} \\ w_b\,\bar{y}(\lambda_b) + w_g\,\bar{y}(\lambda_g) + w_r\,\bar{y}(\lambda_r) &= Y^{*} \\ w_b\,\bar{z}(\lambda_b) + w_g\,\bar{z}(\lambda_g) + w_r\,\bar{z}(\lambda_r) &= Z^{*} \end{aligned}
blocks.caption: X*, Y*, Z* are the target tristimulus values the constructed metamer must match. They come from integrating the reference spectrum against the CIE matching functions. λ_b, λ_g, λ_r are three chosen peak wavelengths, here 460, 540, and 600 nm. w_b, w_g, w_r are the three unknown peak heights to be solved for. x̄, ȳ, z̄ are the CIE matching functions evaluated at each chosen wavelength, fixed numbers tabulated in the 1931 standard.

blocks.text: The 3×3 coefficient matrix is the values of the three matching functions at the three chosen wavelengths. As long as the three wavelengths are well separated and the matrix is nonsingular (which holds for almost any choice in the visible band), the system has a unique solution. The reference spectrum and the three-peak spectrum produce identical (X, Y, Z) by construction and so look identical to the standard observer. They share a color while sharing almost no shape.

blocks.caption: The dashed gray curve is the reference, a Planckian blackbody at 3500 K (a warm tungsten-like white). The rust spikes at 460, 540, and 600 nm are its three-peak metamer, with heights solved live from the 3×3 system above. Drag the t slider to linearly blend between the two spectra. The shape of the lit curve twists from smooth-continuum to three-narrow-line as t walks from 0 to 1. The color swatch and the (X, Y, Z) and (x, y) readouts hold steady, because linear blends of metamers are themselves metamers, by linearity of the integral.

blocks.text: Three peaks is the minimum interesting metamer. Adding a fourth narrow peak gives a one-parameter family of equally valid metamers at the same (X, Y, Z), since one degree of freedom is unconstrained. Adding many peaks gives an infinite family. The black metamers (the silent perturbations) are the differences between any two of them. Every working stage light, phosphor lamp, theatrical filter, and dye recipe lives somewhere inside this geometry.

sections.heading: 4. Why a match in one light fails in another

blocks.text: The dyers and pigment makers Helmholtz quoted were not matching light sources. They were matching surfaces, and surfaces have an intrinsic property: a spectral reflectance R(λ), the fraction of incident light at wavelength λ that the surface scatters back, a number between 0 and 1 at each wavelength. Two pigments with reflectances R₁(λ) and R₂(λ) agree under an illuminant of spectral power distribution I(λ) when the tristimulus integrals of the product I × R agree. Both factors live inside the integral, multiplied together at each wavelength:

blocks.caption: I(λ) is the spectral power distribution of the illuminant, the light striking the surface. R(λ) is the spectral reflectance of the surface, the fraction reflected at each wavelength, a property intrinsic to the pigment. The product I(λ) R(λ) is the spectrum of light leaving the surface and entering the eye. x̄, ȳ, z̄ are the CIE matching functions. X, Y, and Z are the tristimulus values the standard observer reads from that surface under that illuminant.

blocks.text: Two reflectances are metameric under illuminant I if and only if their difference integrates to zero against each illuminant-weighted matching function:

blocks.tex: \int I(\lambda)\,[R_1(\lambda) - R_2(\lambda)]\,\bar{x}(\lambda)\,d\lambda \;=\; \int I(\lambda)\,[R_1(\lambda) - R_2(\lambda)]\,\bar{y}(\lambda)\,d\lambda \;=\; \int I(\lambda)\,[R_1(\lambda) - R_2(\lambda)]\,\bar{z}(\lambda)\,d\lambda \;=\; 0
blocks.caption: R₁(λ) and R₂(λ) are the two surface reflectances under test. I(λ) is the illuminant they are viewed under. The bracket R₁ − R₂ is the wavelength-by-wavelength reflectance difference between the two surfaces. x̄, ȳ, z̄ are the CIE matching functions. The three illuminant-weighted curves I × x̄, I × ȳ, I × z̄ play the role of the matching functions did in the unweighted case. Switching to a different illuminant I' changes these three weighting curves, and the same R₁ − R₂ generally no longer integrates to zero against the new set.

blocks.text: The illuminant lives inside the integral, weighting which wavelengths the test emphasizes. A daylight illuminant I_D65, the noon-daylight standard with a smooth shape across the visible band, weights the wavelengths roughly evenly. A phosphor-converted white LED, with narrow spikes near 450 and 605 nm and almost no light between 470 and 540 nm, gives nearly zero weight to the cyan wavelengths in between. Two reflectances that integrate to the same (X, Y, Z) under D65 may differ by a function whose support sits exactly on the LED's narrow peaks, and the LED then resolves them as distinct. The shop window match becomes a customer-disagreement at home.

The dye industry has lived with this since the loom and the vat. The CIE responded in 1965 with the Color Rendering Index, the subject of the lesson two days ago: rate a test light by how badly its reflectance metamers diverge from those of a Planckian reference on a fixed battery of test reflectances. The Ra score is the average of the divergences. The whole instrument is a measurement of illuminant-dependent metamerism failure, dressed up as a number on a bulb box.

quiz.question: A garment manufacturer matches a fabric swatch to a print in the showroom under D65 daylight. Both swatches read (X, Y, Z) = (35.1, 41.6, 27.8) on the spectrophotometer. The customer wears the swatch home and judges it against the print under a phosphor-converted white LED of correlated color temperature 4000 K and Color Rendering Index Ra = 85, and complains that they no longer match. Which statement best diagnoses the mismatch?
options: The customer's eye has shifted its chromatic adaptation state from the showroom to the home and is now perceiving the swatch differently; both swatches still have identical (X, Y, Z) under the LED, and the perceived disagreement is purely an adaptation artifact.

The two swatches have different spectral reflectance functions R₁(λ) and R₂(λ) that integrate to the same (X, Y, Z) under D65 but to different (X, Y, Z) under the LED, because the illuminant lives inside the integral. The phosphor LED's narrow spikes weight wavelengths the D65 sun weights smoothly, so a reflectance difference invisible under D65 becomes a measurable Δ(X, Y, Z) under the LED. The CRI of 85 quantifies exactly this expected divergence on a battery of test samples, and a real-world fabric pair is one more sample in the same family.
The LED's flicker at 100 Hz beats with the customer's saccade rate and induces a perceived color shift that disappears when the customer holds still.
The two swatches share R(λ), but the dye on one of them photodegraded during the trip home; the (X, Y, Z) match is now broken because R₁(λ) has drifted, not because the illuminant changed.
quiz.explanation: Option 1 confuses chromatic adaptation (the eye's white-point shift) with illuminant metamerism. Even after full adaptation, an integral with different weighting curves gives different results; the math is independent of the observer's adaptation state. Option 3 is a flicker fairy tale: modern fluorescents and LEDs do flicker, but the relevant perceptual artifacts are subliminal at 100 Hz, not color-shift-producing. Option 4 invents a chemistry incident with no support. The honest answer is option 2 and it is the central catch of illuminant-dependent metamerism. The integrals defining (X, Y, Z) carry I(λ) inside them. Two reflectances tuned to agree on three integrals against one illuminant generally fail to agree on the same three integrals against a different illuminant. A high CRI (Ra = 85) only says the average divergence on the CIE's standard reflectance battery is moderate; a particular fabric pair may sit on the worst-case axis of that divergence and disagree quite visibly.

sections.heading: 5. Maxwell's three filters and the camera with its own null space

blocks.text: James Clerk Maxwell, the Scottish physicist whose 1864 paper on the dynamical theory of the electromagnetic field unified electricity and magnetism, gave a lecture at the Royal Institution in London in May 1861 with an unusual closing demonstration. Three black-and-white photographs of a tartan ribbon had been taken in succession through three liquid filters: red, green, blue. Maxwell projected the three plates simultaneously through the same three filters, in register, onto a screen. The audience saw the ribbon in color. It was the first projected color photograph, and it worked partly by analogy and partly by accident.

Maxwell's photographic emulsion was a wet collodion process sensitive only in the blue and ultraviolet. The red filter let almost no actinic light through to the plate at all. The 'red' record actually captured ultraviolet fluorescence emitted by the ribbon's red dyes, which happened to correlate with their visual redness. The demonstration succeeded by an accidental mechanism that the chemistry of the era could not have explained. James Clerk Maxwell Jr., the original Maxwell, was a careful theorist; the photographic chemistry got there ahead of his physical theory of color photography, and the historical lesson is that the first version of a working principle is often right for the wrong reason and survives long enough to be repaired.

A modern camera does the three-channel projection Maxwell intended, with three filter responses R̄(λ), Ḡ(λ), B̄(λ) baked into the Bayer mosaic (the alternating red, green, green, blue color filter array printed over every CMOS sensor since the late 1970s). The integral takes exactly the same shape as the eye's, with the camera's spectral sensitivities playing the role of x̄, ȳ, z̄:

blocks.caption: R, G, B are the three raw camera signals at one pixel after demosaicing and before any color matrix. I(λ) is the scene illuminant. R(λ) is the spectral reflectance of the scene point being imaged (the same R as in section 4, distinct from the camera channel R on the left side). R̄(λ), Ḡ(λ), and B̄(λ) are the camera's red, green, and blue filter spectral sensitivities, fixed by the sensor manufacturer and shaped by the dye chemistry of the Bayer filters and the silicon quantum efficiency. The integrals run over the visible band.

blocks.text: Robert Luther in 1927 and Herbert Ives in 1929 independently wrote down the condition under which a camera can match the eye's color judgments exactly through a fixed 3×3 matrix. The camera's R̄, Ḡ, B̄ must be linear combinations of the eye's x̄, ȳ, z̄. This is the Luther-Ives condition, and no commercial camera sensor satisfies it. The 3×3 color matrix that every raw converter applies after Bayer demosaicing is a least-squares approximation tuned per camera against an X-Rite ColorChecker (the 24-patch reflectance target used by every camera-calibration lab since 1976). Where the camera's filters live in a different part of the spectrum from the eye's matching functions, the matrix cannot rescue the difference.

The asymmetry it leaves behind is the modern catch behind every camera. Two reflectances metameric to the eye need not be metameric to the camera; two reflectances metameric to the camera need not be metameric to the eye. The kernels have moved. Hyperspectral imaging, which captures 30 to 300 wavelength bands per pixel rather than three, is the answer for jobs that cannot afford the asymmetry: forensic art conservation, agricultural disease scanning, satellite mineralogy. The decision to keep three channels and call them enough was, and remains, an economic choice the eye made for free and the camera engineer keeps paying for.

finalQuiz.question: A camera reviewer writes that the new mirrorless body 'reproduces D65-illuminated color targets to ΔE_ab < 1.5 on the X-Rite ColorChecker after applying the in-camera DCP profile, which is essentially a perfect match.' The reviewer then complains that the same camera, photographing the same Crayola crayon collection under a phosphor-converted white LED desk lamp (Ra = 82), produces visibly different reds and oranges than the eye sees. What is the deepest reason for the second observation, and how does it relate to the first?
options: The LED's CRI of 82 is lower than the D65 reference of 100, so the camera's effective color accuracy degrades by exactly 82/100 = 0.82, and the reviewer's complaint is the inevitable arithmetic of the CRI specification.

The camera's spectral sensitivities R̄(λ), Ḡ(λ), B̄(λ) are not linear combinations of the CIE matching functions x̄(λ), ȳ(λ), z̄(λ), violating the Luther-Ives condition. Under D65 the 3×3 color matrix, fitted against the ColorChecker, brings the camera's readings into agreement with the eye's by least-squares overfit on that specific reflectance set. Under the phosphor LED, the illuminant's narrow spikes weight wavelengths where the camera filters and the eye disagree, exciting the camera-eye observer-metamerism kernel. The same 3×3 matrix cannot correct pairwise reflectance differences that vanish for the eye but survive for the camera, because the matrix is a linear operator on three numbers, and the failure is structural, not a tuning problem.

The camera's autoexposure is biased toward the average ColorChecker luminance under D65 and underexposes under the dimmer phosphor LED, and the resulting tone-curve compression mangles the reds and oranges; replacing the camera matrix with one tuned for the LED's chromaticity fixes it.

The Bayer mosaic interpolates one R pixel from neighbors of two G pixels, so under the narrow red phosphor peak of the LED the demosaicing introduces chromatic aliasing that the 3×3 matrix cannot fix; the answer is a higher-resolution sensor.
finalQuiz.explanation: Option 1 confuses CRI (a measurement of an illuminant's effect on an eye-versus-Planckian metamer test) with camera color accuracy under that illuminant. The two are related but not arithmetically convertible, and a camera's color matrix has its own per-illuminant performance independent of CRI. Option 3 invents an autoexposure failure to explain a structural color error; tone reproduction and color reproduction are separate problems. Option 4 invokes demosaicing artifacts that real cameras do encounter but that produce spatial color noise, not the bulk red-orange shift the reviewer describes. The honest answer is option 2 and it is exactly the observer-metamerism mismatch this lesson is about. The Luther-Ives condition says a camera can match the eye's color judgments exactly through a fixed 3×3 matrix only if its filters are linear combinations of the eye's matching functions. No commercial sensor satisfies it; the matrix in the raw converter is a least-squares fit, accurate where the fitting reflectances are and approximate everywhere else. Under D65, the ColorChecker fit happens to produce small ΔE_ab errors on the patches it was trained on. Under the phosphor LED, the illuminant's narrow spectral shape excites parts of the camera-eye disagreement that the matrix cannot reach, and the reds and oranges, dependent on a wavelength band where camera and eye diverge most, are exactly where the failure shows up first.
